clusters.title: Scientists just watched Alzheimer’s damage happen in real time
clusters.summary: Scientists at Oregon State University have captured something researchers have long struggled to see: the real-time chemical interactions that help drive Alzheimer’s disease. By watching how metal ions—especially copper—trigger harmful protein clumping in the brain, the team uncovered a clearer picture of how the disease develops at a molecular level.

clusters.title: Listening to bad music makes you crave sugar, study finds
clusters.summary: An experimental study found that listening to disliked music decreases a person's general desire to eat, but specifically increases their preference for high-sugar foods, likely as a way to cope with the negative mood induced by the audio.

items.title: Listening to bad music makes you crave sugar, study finds

clusters.title: Your vitamin D levels in midlife could shape your brain decades later
clusters.summary: Vitamin D levels in midlife may play a bigger role in long-term brain health than previously thought. In a study following nearly 800 people over 16 years, those with higher vitamin D levels in their 30s and 40s had lower levels of tau protein later on, a key marker linked to dementia.

items.title: Your vitamin D levels in midlife could shape your brain decades later

clusters.title: Scientists were wrong about lifespan. Your genes matter way more than we thought
clusters.summary: For years, scientists believed our lifespan was mostly shaped by environment and chance, with genetics playing only a minor role. But a new study from the Weizmann Institute flips that idea on its head, revealing that genes may actually account for about half of the differences in how long people live. By analyzing massive twin datasets—including twins raised apart—and using innovative simulations to filter out deaths from accidents and other external causes, researchers uncovered a hidden genetic influence that had been masked for decades.

clusters.title: Will Insurance Cover Ozempic for Sleep Apnea?
clusters.summary: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1s) like Ozempic can help with weight loss, which may assist with sleep apnea symptoms. If your sleep apnea has worsened with weight gain or you have other medical conditions that warrant the use of GLP-1s, your healthcare provider may prescribe one, but they can be pricey. clusters.title: Scientists discover why bread can cause weight gain without extra calories
clusters.summary: Bread and other carbohydrate staples may be doing more than just filling plates—they could be quietly reshaping metabolism. In a surprising twist, researchers found that mice strongly preferred carbs like bread, rice, and wheat, abandoning their regular diet entirely. Even without eating more calories, they gained weight and body fat, not because they overate, but because their bodies burned less energy.

clusters.title: Diet tips during cancer treatment
clusters.summary: Cancer treatments can take a toll on a person's body. A patient's treatment may cause nausea, changes in appetite, taste and smell, diarrhea, or constipation, making it harder to meet their nutritional needs. Fortunately, there are strategies that patients and caregivers can use to cope with these side effects.

clusters.title: Millions start work too early. This drug helps them stay awake
clusters.summary: Millions of people start work before sunrise—but their brains aren’t ready for it. A new clinical trial has found that the wake-promoting drug solriamfetol can significantly boost alertness in early-morning shift workers struggling with shift work disorder. Participants who took the drug were able to stay awake and function better throughout full shifts, with improvements in productivity, safety, and daily performance.

clusters.title: Dragonflies can see a color humans can’t and it could change medicine
clusters.summary: Dragonflies may see the world in a way that pushes beyond human limits—and surprisingly, they do it using the same molecular trick we evolved ourselves. Scientists discovered that these insects can detect extremely deep red light, even edging into near-infrared, thanks to a specialized visual protein strikingly similar to the one in human eyes. This ability likely helps them spot mates mid-flight by picking up subtle differences in reflected light.

clusters.title: Improving oral care more than halves hospital-acquired pneumonia risk, major trial finds
clusters.summary: A landmark trial presented at ESCMID Global 2026 shows that improving oral hygiene for hospital patients can reduce the risk of non-ventilator-associated hospital-acquired pneumonia (NV-HAP) by 60%.

clusters.title: Cardiorespiratory fitness may cut dementia, depression and psychosis risk
clusters.summary: Many studies carried out over the past decades have explored the relationship between mental and physical health, showing that the two are often interlinked. One well-established indicator of overall physical health is cardiorespiratory fitness, which is the ability of the heart and lungs to supply oxygen while a person is engaged in sustained physical activity.

clusters.title: After high school, exercise collapses for one in three young adults as screens and disinterest take over
clusters.summary: Younger children and teens often have built-in opportunities to stay active through gym class, sports, and extracurricular activities. But after high school, those structured options often disappear. And many young adults struggle to stay active during this transition, a national poll suggests.

items.title: After high school, exercise collapses for one in three young adults as screens and disinterest take over

clusters.title: Fathers face rising depression risk a year after baby arrives
clusters.summary: New fathers appear to have fewer mental health diagnoses during pregnancy and the early months after birth. But that early stability does not last. About a year later, depression and stress-related disorders increase significantly, surprising researchers.

items.title: Fathers face rising depression risk a year after baby arrives
items.description: New fathers appear to have fewer mental health diagnoses during pregnancy and the early months after birth. But that early stability does not last. About a year later, depression and stress-related disorders increase significantly, surprising researchers. The findings suggest that the emotional toll of fatherhood builds over time rather than hitting immediately.

clusters.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
clusters.summary: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation.

items.title: Depression may start with an energy problem in brain cells
items.description: Researchers have discovered a surprising change in how cells produce energy in people with depression. Brain and blood cells in young adults with major depressive disorder produced more energy molecules at rest but had trouble increasing energy production when needed. Scientists believe this imbalance may contribute to symptoms such as fatigue and low motivation. The finding could help pave the way for earlier diagnosis and more personalized treatments.

clusters.title: Scientists discover sleep switch that builds muscle, burns fat, and boosts brainpower
clusters.summary: Deep sleep does far more than rest the body — it activates a powerful brain-driven system that controls growth hormone, fueling muscle and bone strength, metabolism, and even mental performance. Scientists have now mapped the neural circuits behind this process, uncovering a delicate feedback loop in which sleep boosts growth hormone, and that same hormone helps regulate wakefulness.

clusters.title: New study reveals how political bias conditions the impact of conspiracy thinking
clusters.summary: A person’s tendency to see secret plots strongly predicts their belief in political rumors. But new research suggests this trait has limits: people quickly dismiss conspiracies if the rumors make their own political allies look bad.

items.title: New study reveals how political bias conditions the impact of conspiracy thinking
items.description: A person’s tendency to see secret plots strongly predicts their belief in political rumors. But new research suggests this trait has limits: people quickly dismiss conspiracies if the rumors make their own political allies look bad.

clusters.title: Exercise and ibuprofen may lessen cancer-related cognitive impairment
clusters.summary: Up to 80% of people who receive chemotherapy experience cancer-related cognitive impairment, which most commonly involves mild-to-moderate changes such as difficulty paying attention, memory lapses, and struggles with multitasking. A new Phase II trial has found that exercise and low-dose ibuprofen can each help to lessen cognitive problems and help protect patients' cognitive function. The findings are published in Cancer.

clusters.title: Eating more meat may lower Alzheimer’s risk for some people
clusters.summary: A surprising new study suggests that genetics may change how diet affects brain health—especially when it comes to Alzheimer’s risk. Researchers found that older adults carrying high-risk APOE gene variants didn’t show the expected cognitive decline if they ate relatively high amounts of meat. In fact, those with these genes who consumed the most meat had slower cognitive decline and lower dementia risk, challenging conventional dietary advice.

clusters.title: Goodbye colonoscopy? New stool test detects 90% of colorectal cancers
clusters.summary: A breakthrough in microbiome research could change how colorectal cancer is detected—no colonoscopy required. Scientists used AI to map gut bacteria at an unprecedented level of detail, revealing subtle microbial patterns linked to cancer. By analyzing simple stool samples, their method identified 90% of cases, rivaling one of medicine’s most trusted diagnostic tools.

clusters.title: Your nose could detect Alzheimer’s years before symptoms begin
clusters.summary: Losing your sense of smell might signal Alzheimer’s far earlier than expected. Scientists found that immune cells in the brain actively destroy smell-related nerve fibers after detecting abnormal signals on their surfaces. This damage begins in early stages of the disease, well before cognitive decline.

items.title: Your nose could detect Alzheimer’s years before symptoms begin
items.description: Losing your sense of smell might signal Alzheimer’s far earlier than expected. Scientists found that immune cells in the brain actively destroy smell-related nerve fibers after detecting abnormal signals on their surfaces. This damage begins in early stages of the disease, well before cognitive decline. The discovery could help identify at-risk patients sooner and improve treatment timing.
